docs.title: Docs Index
docs.text: This docs folder mirrors the Python SDK structure where it makes sense, but the content here is specific to the JS SDK project and this native-first checkout.

docs.title: Current Branch Reality
docs.text:
• Native high-level agent optimization is supported.
  • Backend-guided local execution through the typed session surface is supported.
  • Hybrid session helpers are supported for:
  • create
  • list
  • status
  • finalize
  • delete
  • seamless now covers:
  • framework interception for wrapped OpenAI, LangChain, and Vercel AI clients
  • codemod/build-time rewriting for hardcoded tuned variables
  • an experimental runtime rewrite fallback for self-contained plain Node functions when explicitly opted in
  • Python-style remote cloud execution remains out-of-scope for JS.

docs.title: Project-Wide Note
docs.text: The parity docs still distinguish native-first behavior from backend-guided hybrid behavior when the constraints or guarantees differ. In this repository root, both surfaces are now present and documented as one merged SDK.

docs.title: Reference Material
docs.text:
• REAL_MODE_SEQUENCE_FLOW.md is retained only as an archival bridge reference for Python SDK <= 0.11.x.
  • Mermaid diagrams under docs/diagrams/ describe the older bridge/orchestrated flow, not the native-first JS API or Python SDK 0.12.0+ behavior.

docs.title: Branch Protection
docs.text:
Repository settings are not managed from code, but main should be protected with these required checks:

  • CI / lint-and-typecheck
  • CI / test (node 18)
  • CI / test (node 20)
  • CI / test (node 22)
  • CI / build (node 18)
  • CI / build (node 20)
  • CI / build (node 22)
  • CI / package-smoke (node 18)
  • CI / package-smoke (node 20)
  • CI / package-smoke (node 22)
  • Changeset Required / changeset-required
  • CodeQL / Analyze

The nightly/manual Hybrid Backend Smoke workflow is valuable operational coverage, but it is not a required branch-protection check because it depends on external backend secrets and infrastructure.

Recommended additional settings:

  • require pull request before merging
  • require at least 1 approving review
  • dismiss stale approvals on new commits
  • require branches to be up to date before merging
  • restrict direct pushes to main
  • require CODEOWNERS review if a CODEOWNERS file is later added

docs.title: Client Code Guide
docs.text: This guide describes the current JavaScript and TypeScript integration surface for @traigent/sdk in this checkout.

docs.title: Choose a Flow
docs.text:
Use the SDK in one of two supported ways:

Flow When to use it Main APIs Hybrid mode authoring Your app exposes Traigent-compatible HTTP routes and needs a code-defined config space optimize, param, toHybridConfigSpace, TrialContext, getTrialParam, getTrialConfig Native Node optimization Your optimization loop should run in-process in Node optimize, param, wrapped.optimize(...), applyBestConfig()

This repo supports call-level backend-guided hybrid Optuna through .optimize({ algorithm: "optuna", ... }); spec-level execution.mode = "hybrid" remains rejected. Python SDK 0.12.0 removed Python-orchestrated JS optimization. The CLI runner remains temporarily for Python SDK <= 0.11.x compatibility and is documented only as an archival reference in REAL_MODE_SEQUENCE_FLOW.md.

docs.title: Flow 1: Hybrid Mode Authoring
docs.text: In hybrid mode authoring, the SDK defines the spec and exposes runtime config access. Your service still owns the HTTP routes, validation, dispatch, and error shaping.

docs.title: What you write
docs.text:
1. A code-defined optimization spec.
  2. A host app that converts the spec with toHybridConfigSpace().
  3. Capability or business logic that runs inside TrialContext.run(...).

docs.title: Minimal example
docs.text:
import { TrialContext, getTrialParam, optimize, param, toHybridConfigSpace } from '@traigent/sdk';

export const childAgeSpec = optimize({
  configurationSpace: {
    model: param.enum(['gpt-4o-mini', 'gpt-4o']),
    temperature: param.float({ min: 0, max: 1, scale: 'linear' }),
    system_prompt_version: param.enum(['v1', 'v2', 'v3']),
    max_retries: param.int({ min: 0, max: 3, scale: 'linear' }),
  },
  objectives: ['accuracy', 'cost'],
  execution: {
    contract: 'trial',
  },
  evaluation: {
    data: [{ id: 1 }],
  },
})(async () => ({
  metrics: {
    accuracy: 0,
    cost: 0,
  },
}));

export const hybridConfigSpace = toHybridConfigSpace(childAgeSpec);

export async function executeCapability(body: {
  request_id?: string;
  session_id?: string;
  config?: Record<string, unknown>;
  inputs: unknown[];
}) {
  const trialConfig = {
    trial_id: body.request_id ?? crypto.randomUUID(),
    trial_number: 0,
    experiment_run_id: body.session_id ?? body.request_id ?? 'hybrid-api',
    config: body.config ?? {},
    dataset_subset: {
      indices: [],
      total: body.inputs.length,
    },
  };

  return TrialContext.run(trialConfig, async () => {
    const model = getTrialParam('model', 'gpt-4o-mini');
    const temperature = getTrialParam('temperature', 0.2);

    return { model, temperature };
  });
}

docs.title: Hybrid authoring rules
docs.text:
• Keep configurationSpace, objectives, budget, evaluation, injection, and execution in camelCase.
  • Keep your existing hybrid wire contract unchanged by serializing with toHybridConfigSpace().
  • Resolve runtime config with getTrialParam() and getTrialConfig() inside the execution path, not in top-level route handlers.
  • If you temporarily support both code-defined specs and JSON config files, choose one source at startup and fail fast when they disagree.

docs.title: Flow 2: Native Node Optimization
docs.text: In native mode, the primary JS contract is a plain agent function plus an evaluation block. The SDK owns dataset iteration, output scoring, metric aggregation, and optimization.

docs.title: What you write
docs.text:
1. A spec with optimize(...).
  2. A plain agent function returning output.
  3. An evaluation dataset supplied via evaluation.data or evaluation.loadData.

docs.title: Minimal example
docs.text:
import { getTrialParam, optimize, param } from '@traigent/sdk';

const answerQuestion = optimize({
  configurationSpace: {
    model: param.enum(['cheap', 'accurate']),
    temperature: param.float({ min: 0, max: 0.5, step: 0.5, scale: 'linear' }),
  },
  objectives: ['accuracy', 'cost'],
  budget: {
    maxCostUsd: 1,
  },
  execution: {
    maxTotalExamples: 100,
    exampleConcurrency: 4,
    repsPerTrial: 3,
    repsAggregation: 'median',
  },
  evaluation: {
    data: [
      { input: 'What is 2+2?', output: '4' },
      { input: 'What is the capital of France?', output: 'Paris' },
    ],
    scoringFunction: (output, expectedOutput) => (output === expectedOutput ? 1 : 0),
    metricFunctions: {
      cost: (_output, _expectedOutput, _runtimeMetrics, row) => {
        const input = String((row as { input?: unknown }).input ?? '');
        return input.includes('capital') ? 0.2 : 0.1;
      },
    },
  },
})(async (question: string) => {
  const model = String(getTrialParam('model', 'cheap'));
  return model === 'accurate' ? (question.includes('capital') ? 'Paris' : '4') : 'unknown';
});

const result = await answerQuestion.optimize({
  algorithm: 'grid',
  maxTrials: 12,
  randomSeed: 42,
  timeoutMs: 5_000,
  trialConcurrency: 2,
});

answerQuestion.applyBestConfig(result);
console.log(await answerQuestion('What is 2+2?'));
console.log(answerQuestion.currentConfig());

docs.title: Injection modes
docs.text:
• context:
  • default mode
  • use getTrialParam() / getTrialConfig() in the wrapped function
  • parameter:
  • the SDK calls your function as agentFn(input, config?)
  • use this when you want explicit tuned-variable flow in user code
  • seamless:
  • first-class through framework wrappers for OpenAI, LangChain, and Vercel AI
  • the fastest framework path is now:
    • autoWrapFrameworkTarget(...) for one client/model
    • autoWrapFrameworkTargets({ ... }) for a small object map of supported targets
    • discoverFrameworkTargets(...) to inspect an explicit object graph
    • prepareFrameworkTargets(...) for one-call discover + wrap + status
  • also supported for hardcoded local tuned variables through:
    • traigent migrate seamless
    • @traigent/sdk/babel-plugin-seamless
    • an experimental runtime rewrite fallback for self-contained plain Node functions when TRAIGENT_ENABLE_EXPERIMENTAL_RUNTIME_SEAMLESS=1
  • defaults autoOverrideFrameworks to true, so all active wrapped targets are eligible unless you narrow them with frameworkTargets
  • fail-closed semantics:
    • the codemod reports rejected patterns and will not modify blocked files
    • the Babel plugin throws instead of emitting a partial transform
  • frameworkTargets is still useful when you want to constrain seamless interception to specific SDKs
  • set autoOverrideFrameworks: false when you want seamless to ignore active wrapped SDK targets and rely only on transformed code paths

docs.title: Tuned-variable discovery
docs.text:
The native checkout now includes a bounded tuned-variable discovery helper for local JS functions:

import { discoverTunedVariables } from '@traigent/sdk';

const report = discoverTunedVariables(answerQuestion);
console.log(report.candidates);

Use it when you want a heuristic report of likely tunable locals before moving to explicit context / parameter injection or the seamless codemod path.

The matching CLI is:

traigent detect tuned-variables src/agent.ts --function answerQuestion

For native TVL artifacts, the CLI can also inspect the loaded feature set and native-compatibility report:

traigent inspect tvl path/to/spec.yml

Current scope:

  • native checkout only
  • self-contained function bodies
  • literal defaults that map to the JS config-space model
  • high/medium/low confidence reporting with warnings for skipped reassigned vars

This is intentionally still behind Python’s fuller tuned-variable discovery and config-generation pipeline.

Example:

import {
  describeFrameworkAutoOverride,
  prepareFrameworkTargets,
  optimize,
  param,
} from '@traigent/sdk';

const prepared = prepareFrameworkTargets({
  openaiClient,
  chatModel,
});
const wrapped = prepared.wrapped;

const agent = optimize({
  configurationSpace: {
    model: param.enum(['gpt-4o-mini', 'gpt-4o']),
    temperature: param.float({ min: 0, max: 1, step: 0.2, scale: 'linear' }),
  },
  objectives: ['accuracy'],
  injection: {
    mode: 'seamless',
  },
  evaluation: {
    data: [{ input: 'hello', output: 'ok' }],
    scoringFunction: () => 1,
  },
})(async (input: string) => wrapped.chatModel.invoke(input));

console.log(describeFrameworkAutoOverride(undefined, true));
console.log(prepared.discovered);

docs.title: Bounded framework discovery
docs.text:
The native checkout now also supports bounded framework discovery for explicit object graphs:

import { discoverFrameworkTargets, prepareFrameworkTargets } from '@traigent/sdk';

const discovered = discoverFrameworkTargets({
  services: { openaiClient, chatModel },
});

const prepared = prepareFrameworkTargets({
  services: { openaiClient, chatModel },
});

Current scope:

  • direct supported targets
  • nested arrays and plain-object graphs
  • cycle-safe traversal

Out of scope:

  • scanning module globals
  • auto-patching arbitrary imported clients you did not pass explicitly

Identity note:

  • OpenAI clients are wrapped in place
  • LangChain and Vercel AI targets return wrapped objects
  • all wrapper paths are idempotent, so repeated wrapping is safe

docs.title: Native mode rules
docs.text:
• Built-in objective strings are limited to accuracy, cost, and latency.
  • Any other objective must use { metric, direction, weight? }.
  • budget.maxCostUsd only works when trials produce numeric metrics.total_cost or metrics.cost.
  • execution.maxTotalExamples caps the total number of evaluated examples across all trials.
  • execution.exampleConcurrency runs multiple examples from the same trial in parallel while preserving stable aggregation and metric-sample order.
  • execution.repsPerTrial and execution.repsAggregation provide repetition-based stability for noisy evaluations.
  • execution.contract = 'trial' still supports the old low-level trial-function style, but it is deprecated and documented as advanced-only.

docs.title: Advanced low-level trial contract
docs.text:
If you intentionally want to manage metrics yourself, you can still opt into the old contract:

import { optimize, param, type TrialConfig } from '@traigent/sdk';

const runTrial = optimize({
  configurationSpace: {
    model: param.enum(['cheap', 'accurate']),
  },
  objectives: ['accuracy'],
  execution: {
    contract: 'trial',
  },
  evaluation: {
    data: [{ id: 1 }],
  },
})(async (trialConfig: TrialConfig) => ({
  metrics: {
    accuracy: trialConfig.config.model === 'accurate' ? 1 : 0.5,
  },
}));

Treat that as an advanced compatibility path, not the primary JS usage model.

docs.title: Runtime Access Rules
docs.text:
getTrialConfig() and getTrialParam() are the canonical runtime accessors.

Use them only when a trial context is active:

  • inside TrialContext.run(...)
  • inside a native .optimize(...) run
  • during normal calls after applyBestConfig() when using context-based injection
  • inside host-managed capability logic that has already bound a synthetic TrialConfig

Do not introduce a second global config mechanism for JS.

docs.title: Current Checkout Limits
docs.text:
• Native optimization supports grid, random, and sequential bayesian.
  • Native optimization is Node-only.
  • budget.maxCostUsd still depends on numeric metrics.total_cost or metrics.cost.
  • Cost-aware wrappers record input_cost, output_cost, total_cost, and cost in runtime metrics.
  • Log-scale grid search requires a multiplicative step > 1.
  • trialConcurrency is supported for grid and random only.
  • Call-level backend-guided hybrid Optuna is supported through .optimize({ algorithm: "optuna", ... }); spec-level execution.mode = "hybrid" remains rejected.
  • seamless prefers explicit rewrite tooling over runtime magic:
  • use framework wrappers for SDK-mediated params
  • use the codemod or Babel plugin for hardcoded locals
  • treat runtime rewriting as experimental
  • inspect wrapped.seamlessResolution() when you need to confirm which path was actually selected
  • Native JS supports defaultConfig, callback-based constraints, and callback-based safetyConstraints.
  • TVL loading is supported for a focused native subset:
  • typed tvars
  • banded objectives
  • structural and derived constraints compiled through a parsed safe-expression subset
  • exploration strategy/budget mapping
  • promotion-policy parsing
  • parseTvlSpec() and loadTvlSpec() expose artifact-specific nativeCompatibility so the supported vs reduced-semantics native envelope is explicit in the loaded file, including usedFeatures and summarized warnings
  • Promotion policy is partially enforced in this checkout: native best-trial selection honors minEffect and tieBreakers, uses statistical promotion when both trials expose per-objective metric samples, and chanceConstraints reject trials when they have explicit {successes, trials} counts or binary metric samples. Native results/trials now expose bounded promotionDecision reports plus a small result.reporting summary for trial counts, evaluated examples, and whether chance constraints / statistical comparison / tie-breakers were involved. Full Python promotion-gate lifecycle parity is still deferred.
  • Python's safety preset/statistical layer is still ahead of this checkout; backend-guided Optuna exists through the typed session path, while Python's server-side remote execution model remains out of scope for JS.

docs.title: Archived Bridge Reference
docs.text:
The Python-orchestrated bridge is no longer supported by Python SDK 0.12.0 and later. The traigent-js runner remains temporarily for Python SDK <= 0.11.x compatibility. New JS integrations should use native optimize(spec)(fn) or hybrid spec authoring.

Stage 2 removal trigger: remove the runner only after release owners confirm no production Python SDK <= 0.11.x workloads still depend on it, with removal no earlier than @traigent/sdk 1.0.

docs.title: Cross-SDK Validation
docs.text: Native JS correctness and performance are validated against Python-owned fixtures and benchmark specs in the sibling Traigent repo.

docs.title: Correctness Oracles
docs.text:
Python exports deterministic oracle payloads from:

  • ../Traigent/tests/cross_sdk_oracles/generate_native_js_oracles.py

The JS parity tests consume that payload in:

  • tests/cross-sdk/oracle.test.ts

Covered oracle cases:

  • grid_3x3: exact config order, best config, best metrics, normalized stop reason
  • random_seed_42: exact seeded config sequence for deterministic discrete sampling
  • budget_cutoff: same budget stop point, total cost, best config, normalized stop reason
  • bayesian_branin: optional outcome-envelope check when the Python Bayesian environment is available

If the sibling Python repo is absent, the cross-SDK oracle test skips locally. Internal release validation must run it.

docs.title: Async Benchmark Methodology
docs.text:
Cross-language async comparison uses a synthetic workload, not real provider calls.

Shared benchmark inputs come from:

  • ../Traigent/tests/cross_sdk_oracles/generate_native_js_oracles.py
  • ../Traigent/tests/cross_sdk_oracles/run_async_scheduler_benchmark.py
  • scripts/run-cross-sdk-benchmark.mjs

Method:

  1. Use the same seeded random config space and the same sleep schedule in both runtimes.
  2. Run warmups first, then measured runs at concurrency 1, 2, 4, and 8.
  3. Compare normalized scheduler behavior rather than absolute language speed.

Reported metrics:

  • wall-clock time
  • throughput
  • theoretical minimum makespan
  • normalized overhead ratio
  • duplicate-config rate
  • context-leak count
  • RSS delta
  • best config
  • best latency
  • stop reason
  • trial count

docs.title: Commands
docs.text:
npm run check:schema-types
npm run check:version
npm run test:cross-sdk
npm run benchmark:cross-sdk

The benchmark command builds the SDK, runs the JS benchmark harness, executes the Python benchmark script, and prints a combined JSON report.

docs.title: Hybrid Session Contract Checks
docs.text:
Hybrid JS optimization adds mocked contract validation around the Python backend session API:

  • session creation payload shape
  • next-trial suggestion decoding
  • result submission payload shape
  • stop-reason normalization

These checks live in:

  • tests/unit/optimization/hybrid.test.ts

docs.title: Manifest-Backed Product Client Parity Checks
docs.text:
The authoritative Python/JS parity manifest is owned by TraigentSchema:

  • ../TraigentSchema/parity/python-js-sdk.json

CI checks out TraigentSchema at an explicit release tag rather than main so schema changes do not silently change JavaScript CI. Refresh that pinned ref when a new schema release is intentionally adopted by the JS SDK.

For JS 0.2.0 it pins Python origin/develop at f9f1adcb19ea8c3874bb1b7ef21f6d11b1b95a18. It intentionally does not pin a JS HEAD SHA; every JS build is valid if it satisfies the manifest-required exports. JS 0.2.0 may ship before Python 0.12.0, but if it does, release notes must call that out as forward-compatible parity with the next Python surface rather than parity with currently published Python 0.11.4.

The product client surface is generated from the canonical ../TraigentSchema/traigent_schema/schemas contracts and exposed through:

  • @traigent/sdk/projects
  • @traigent/sdk/prompts
  • @traigent/sdk/evaluation
  • @traigent/sdk/observability
  • @traigent/sdk/core-metrics
  • @traigent/sdk/admin
  • @traigent/sdk/schema

npm run check:schema-types fails when generated TS contract types drift from TraigentSchema. npm run check:parity-manifest fails when the JS package root does not expose the symbols TraigentSchema currently requires for Python develop parity. npm run check:version keeps runtime telemetry headers aligned with package.json. The package smoke suite also imports every new subpath through both ESM and CJS so package export drift is caught before release.

The older Python-side ignore-list guard should be replaced by this manifest contract rather than kept as a second source of truth. Python CI can still read the JS API snapshot, but TraigentSchema owns the per-symbol classification: matched, additive-js-alias, deferred-schema, deferred-backlog, or out-of-scope-permanent.

Current explicit 0.2.0 deferrals:

  • BenchmarkClient is a schema-deferred JS stub until the dataset-generation endpoint is schema-owned (Traigent/traigent-js#31).
  • EnterpriseAdminClient remains a JS migration stub pending Traigent/traigent-js#30. Enterprise admin operations are REST-only in 0.2.x.
  • observability default-client helpers (get_default_observability_client, set_default_observability_client) and the observe wrapper are deferred to 0.3.0 at the earliest (Traigent/traigent-js#38); none of them are exported as stubs in 0.2.x.
  • catalog recommendation APIs, strategy presets, add_agent_span-style instrumentation helpers, advisory selection helpers, and the onboarding CLI are deferred backlog items rather than JS 0.2.x parity claims.

The contract-policy detail and release-gating criteria for these deferred surfaces live in MIGRATION_FROM_PYTHON.md. A JS release that flips any of them from deferred-schema / deferred-backlog to matched in ../TraigentSchema/parity/python-js-sdk.json must land the schema, the Python __all__ change, the JS implementation, and the manifest reclassification together. The parity-manifest gate (npm run check:parity-manifest) is the load-bearing check that keeps these three surfaces aligned across SDKs.

Manifest refresh cadence:

  • every Python public SDK release candidate or release branch
  • every Python PR family that changes traigent.__all__
  • before every JS minor parity release
  • quarterly drift check if neither SDK has released in the previous quarter

docs.title: Hybrid JS Parity Matrix
docs.text:
This matrix describes the verified state of the hybrid/session surface in this checkout.

Use it together with:

  • Native JS Parity Matrix
  • Python SDK Module Catalog and Gap Analysis

docs.title: Label Semantics
docs.text:
• matched: implemented and covered by passing tests or a verified public example
  • partial: implemented with bounded semantics and covered, but still behind Python
  • gap: the backend route is reachable today and the JS side could implement it now
  • deferred-backend: blocked on missing or insufficiently specified backend/protocol support
  • out-of-scope: not a current JS SDK target

Scope note:

  • this matrix treats backend-guided local execution over the typed /sessions surface as the JS parity target
  • Python's separate server-side remote cloud-execution family is not treated as a JS gap here

Hybrid session lifecycle Yes matched tests/unit/optimization/hybrid.test.ts Create → next-trial → submit-result → finalize is implemented and tested. Hybrid cost/budget handling Yes partial tests/unit/optimization/hybrid.test.ts Budget enforcement works, but still expects the current backend/client cost metric conventions.

Implicit framework discovery Yes gap n/a The SDK can now discover wrappable targets inside explicitly passed object graphs, but it still does not scan arbitrary module/global state or auto-patch unknown imports.

docs.title: TVL / Spec Compatibility
docs.text: Capability Python Hybrid JS Evidence Notes Typed objective objects Yes matched tests/unit/optimization/spec.test.ts, tests/unit/optimization/hybrid.test.ts Objective objects, weights, and hybrid serialization are implemented. Bounded TVL support Yes partial tests/unit/optimization/tvl.test.ts TVL support exists, but the hybrid/session surface still inherits the same bounded subset strategy as the native checkout. Full TVL/runtime/control-plane parity Yes deferred-backend n/a Requires broader backend/runtime alignment than the current session surface provides.

docs.title: Biggest Remaining Gaps
docs.text: Capability Family Current Status Why It Is Still Open Additional reachable typed /sessions control plane partial The core typed /sessions helper surface is covered. Any remaining JS-side cloud work should be limited to additional routes that are actually reachable from the current backend session base and have a known contract. Python remote cloud-execution family (/agent/optimize, remote invocation) out-of-scope JS hybrid intentionally keeps agent execution local and does not target server-side agent reconstruction/invocation. Implicit framework discovery gap No backend work is required, but the hybrid/session surface still requires callers to pass explicit objects for discovery rather than scanning arbitrary runtime/module state. Full TVL/runtime breadth deferred-backend The backend/session contract does not yet expose the whole Python TVL lifecycle.

docs.title: Internal Release Notes: Native JS Optimization
Scope
docs.text: This release hardens the current native JS optimization surface in @traigent/sdk for this native-first checkout.

docs.title: SDK Changes
docs.text:
• Root exports now include optimize, param, getOptimizationSpec, and toHybridConfigSpace.
  • Wrapped functions now support a high-level agent contract by default:
  • plain agent function returns output
  • SDK-owned evaluation block computes metrics
  • injection modes: context, parameter, seamless
  • Wrapped functions still expose native .optimize({ algorithm, maxTrials, randomSeed, timeoutMs, trialConcurrency, plateau, checkpoint }).
  • Framework interception is available through explicit wrappers for OpenAI, LangChain, and Vercel AI.
  • Seamless support for hardcoded local tuned variables is now available through:
  • traigent migrate seamless
  • @traigent/sdk/babel-plugin-seamless
  • an experimental runtime rewrite fallback for self-contained plain Node functions when explicitly opted in for trusted local code
  • The old low-level manual trial contract still exists behind execution.contract = 'trial', but it is deprecated.

docs.title: Demo / Migration State
docs.text:
• child-age-agent-a is code-defined and serialized with toHybridConfigSpace().
  • child-age-agent-b remains JSON-backed for one migration cycle.
  • Startup fails when spec-backed and JSON-backed config spaces disagree.
  • Runtime config is bound only inside capability execution through TrialContext.run(...).

docs.title: Intentional v1 Limits
docs.text:
• Log-scale grid search requires a multiplicative step > 1.
  • trialConcurrency is currently limited to grid and random.
  • Call-level backend-guided hybrid Optuna is implemented through .optimize({ algorithm: "optuna", ... }); spec-level execution.mode = "hybrid" is still rejected in this checkout.
  • seamless is no longer framework-only, but full Python-style runtime AST parity is still not implemented.
  • No worker pools or Python server-side remote execution in the native runner; Optuna-family behavior is available through backend-guided typed sessions, not local native algorithms.
  • budget.maxCostUsd requires numeric metrics.total_cost or metrics.cost on every trial.
  • Native JS now supports defaultConfig, callback-based constraints, and callback-based safetyConstraints.
  • TVL loading is implemented for a focused native subset:
  • typed tvars
  • banded objectives
  • structural and derived constraints via a parsed safe-expression subset
  • exploration strategy and budget mapping
  • promotion-policy parsing
  • Promotion policy is now partially behavioral in this branch: minEffect and tieBreakers influence native best-trial selection, per-objective metric samples enable statistical promotion for standard and banded objectives, and chanceConstraints reject trials when they expose explicit {successes, trials} counts or binary metric samples. Full Python promotion-gate parity remains deferred.
  • Python's safety preset/statistical layer is still ahead of this checkout. Backend-guided Optuna exists through the typed session path; Python's server-side remote execution model remains out of scope for JS.

docs.title: Release Validation
docs.text:
Run in traigent-js:

npm test
npm run smoke:example
npm run build
npm pack --dry-run

Run in JS-Mastra-APIs-Validation:

npm test
DEMO_MOCK_GENERATION=1 npm run api:dev
npm run api:test

docs.title: Branch Isolation
docs.text: Cut the release from an isolated branch or PR that excludes unrelated local SDK work already present in the traigent-js worktree.

docs.title: JS Parity Roadmap
docs.text:
This roadmap turns the current Python-vs-JS gap analysis into an execution order.

Use this together with:

  • NATIVE_JS_PARITY_MATRIX.md
  • PYTHON_SDK_MODULE_CATALOG_AND_GAP_ANALYSIS.md
  • Hybrid JS parity status is summarized in docs/HYBRID_JS_PARITY_MATRIX.md.

Important branch note:

  • this checkout supports native JS optimization plus call-level backend-guided hybrid Optuna through typed sessions
  • spec-level execution.mode = "hybrid" is still rejected
  • Python's separate server-side remote cloud-execution model is not a JS SDK parity target unless the product direction changes

docs.title: Status Label Rules
docs.text:
• matched: implemented and covered by passing tests or a verified public example
  • partial: implemented with bounded semantics and covered, but still behind Python
  • gap: the backend/API contract is reachable today and the JS side could implement it now
  • deferred-backend: blocked on missing or insufficiently specified backend/protocol support
  • out-of-scope: not a current JS SDK target

The 0.2.0 public root parity gate uses the schema-owned manifest at ../TraigentSchema/parity/python-js-sdk.json. That manifest has a more precise per-symbol vocabulary:

  • matched
  • additive-js-alias
  • deferred-schema
  • deferred-backlog
  • out-of-scope-permanent

It pins Python origin/develop at f9f1adcb19ea8c3874bb1b7ef21f6d11b1b95a18 and intentionally does not pin a JS HEAD SHA.

For 0.2.0, JS may ship before Python 0.12.0. If that happens, the release must describe the manifest as forward-compatible with the next Python public surface, not as parity with published Python 0.11.4.

docs.title: Prioritization Rules
docs.text:
Gaps are ranked by:

  1. user-visible parity impact
  2. whether the gap belongs in this branch
  3. whether the divergence is justified
  4. implementation leverage across multiple Python module families

docs.title: P0: Highest Product Gap, But Not For This Branch
docs.text: These are the largest remaining Python-vs-JS gaps overall, but they should be implemented through reachable backend/session contracts, not by reviving the old Python-orchestrated JS bridge.

docs.title: Backend-guided hybrid execution
docs.text:
• Python status:
  • full cloud/hybrid/session stack
  • includes a separate remote cloud-execution model
  • JS status:
  • call-level backend-guided hybrid Optuna exists through .optimize({ algorithm: "optuna", ... })
  • typed session lifecycle helpers are exported
  • high-level plain-agent local evaluation plus backend-guided config suggestion is implemented
  • seamless framework interception is supported for OpenAI / LangChain / Vercel AI targets
  • spec-level execution.mode = "hybrid" is intentionally rejected here
  • Why it matters:
  • this is the main JS parity path for backend-owned Optuna-like functionality
  • JS parity targets backend-guided local execution over typed /sessions, not Python's server-side remote execution model

docs.title: Cloud/session/control-plane parity
docs.text:
• Python status:
  • remote clients
  • session lifecycle
  • subset selection
  • billing/privacy/auth DTOs
  • JS status:
  • low-level typed session helpers for create / next-trial / submit-result are exported
  • list / health / status / finalize / delete helpers normalize DTOs
  • executable end-to-end session-control examples exist
  • still missing parts of Python's wider cloud/control-plane breadth that are either backend-dependent or intentionally out of scope for JS
  • Why it matters:
  • additional typed /sessions parity should be limited to reachable backend routes with known contracts
  • Python's remote cloud-execution family is not a JS target unless the product direction changes

docs.title: P1: Highest-Value Native Gaps In This Branch
docs.text: These are the most important justified parity gaps that still belong in this native-first checkout.

docs.title: TVL promotion and statistical semantics
docs.text:
• Current JS status:
  • focused TVL loader
  • artifact-specific nativeCompatibility reporting on loaded TVL artifacts, including usedFeatures and warnings
  • banded objectives
  • safe-expression constraint compilation
  • minEffect, tieBreakers
  • sample-based paired promotion
  • TOST-style band promotion
  • behavioral chanceConstraints
  • Remaining gap:
  • no full promotion-gate lifecycle semantics beyond best-trial selection and post-trial rejection
  • no full TVL CLI/models/runtime parity beyond the supported native subset
  • Priority reason:
  • this is the biggest native-local parity gap still left
  • it affects spec compatibility, promotion semantics, and cross-SDK behavior

docs.title: Auto framework override ergonomics
docs.text:
• Current JS status:
  • explicit wrappers exist for OpenAI, LangChain, and Vercel AI
  • seamless mode defaults autoOverrideFrameworks to all active wrapped targets
  • frameworkTargets can narrow active targets and autoOverrideFrameworks: false disables framework interception entirely for a seamless function
  • autoWrapFrameworkTarget(...), autoWrapFrameworkTargets(...), and bounded explicit-object discovery via discoverFrameworkTargets(...) / prepareFrameworkTargets(...) now reduce setup friction for supported framework objects without scanning arbitrary module/global state
  • seamlessResolution() surfaces the resolved seamless path for an optimized function, while describeFrameworkAutoOverride(...) and prepareFrameworkTargets(...).autoOverrideStatus expose active framework target diagnostics
  • Remaining gap:
  • JS still does not auto-discover and wrap framework clients implicitly inside arbitrary user code
  • Guardrails:
  • any future implicit discovery must be opt-in
  • it must not patch globals
  • activation must be visible in logs/diagnostics
  • behavior must be deterministic across import order
  • Priority reason:
  • high user impact
  • closes a real adoption gap without requiring cloud work

docs.title: Broader TVL authoring/runtime parity
docs.text:
• Current JS status:
  • native subset is implemented and tested
  • native CLI inspection now exists via traigent inspect tvl ...
  • Remaining gap:
  • full TVL language/runtime parity
  • tighter alignment with Python authoring expectations
  • Priority reason:
  • important if cross-SDK spec portability is a product requirement

docs.title: P2: Valuable, But Secondary
docs.text: These are useful parity improvements, but they are lower priority than P1.

docs.title: Example concurrency
docs.text:
• Python status:
  • supports broader evaluation parallelism
  • JS status:
  • opt-in per-example concurrency is available through execution.exampleConcurrency
  • Why it is secondary:
  • the bounded local path now exists, but broader Python evaluation parallelism still exceeds this checkout

docs.title: Broader optimizer family parity
docs.text:
• Python status:
  • richer optimizer set, including Optuna-family modes
  • JS status:
  • native grid/random/Bayesian only
  • Why it is secondary:
  • if the long-term strategy is backend-guided hybrid for advanced optimizers, native-local JS does not need to mirror the full Python optimizer family

docs.title: Richer trial taxonomy and pruning semantics
docs.text:
• Python status:
  • more detailed failed/pruned/rejected distinctions
  • JS status:
  • simpler native trial outcome model
  • Why it is secondary:
  • useful for introspection, but not the main user-facing parity blocker

docs.title: Global config access parity
docs.text:
• Python status:
  • global-style get_config() semantics
  • JS status:
  • intentional wrapper-local / context-local model instead
  • Why it is secondary:
  • parity exists as a mental-model difference, but the JS divergence is partly intentional and arguably better aligned with JS runtime behavior

docs.title: P3: Real Python Capabilities, But Not Yet Justified To Copy
docs.text: These appear in the Python audit, but they should not drive near-term JS SDK work in this repo.

docs.title: Platform / enterprise families
docs.text:
• security
  • auth
  • tenancy
  • session hardening
  • analytics/meta-learning

These are platform-side concerns, not core local-SDK parity targets.

docs.title: Assisted generation and discovery families
docs.text:
• bounded tuned-variable discovery is now available in the native checkout through:
  • discoverTunedVariables(...)
  • discoverTunedVariablesFromSource(...)
  • discoverTunedVariablesFromFile(...)
  • traigent detect tuned-variables ...
  • but broader tuned-variable dataflow/range generation and the larger config generation pipelines remain separate product layers from the local native JS optimizer/runtime
  • benchmark/objective recommendation remains out of scope in this checkout
  • the AST parser infrastructure already exists in src/seamless/; the missing tuned-variable work is dataflow/range inference, not new parser plumbing

docs.title: Server / wrapper / adapter families
docs.text:
• wrapper service/server
  • hooks
  • execution adapters
  • bridge/server infrastructure

These should be treated as separate surfaces, not copied blindly into the native-first JS SDK package.

docs.title: Native branch
docs.text:
1. finish TVL promotion/statistical parity
  2. improve auto framework override ergonomics
  3. decide whether full TVL parity is a product requirement or keep a documented supported subset
  4. tighten and benchmark the bounded execution.exampleConcurrency path if runtime throughput becomes a priority

docs.title: Hybrid/session surface
docs.text:
1. extend backend-guided hybrid execution parity where reachable backend routes exist
  2. extend the current typed session helper surface only for reachable backend routes with known contracts
  3. align reachable remote DTO/session contracts with Python
  4. only then revisit broader control-plane or advanced optimizer breadth through the backend path

docs.title: Non-Goals For This Branch
docs.text:
• implementing cloud/session clients directly in this checkout
  • implementing Python-style remote cloud-executed optimization or server-side agent reconstruction in JS
  • copying platform/security/analytics modules into the JS SDK package
  • forcing Python-style global config semantics into JS if the current wrapper- local/context-local design remains clearer

docs.title: 0.2.0 Release Parity Decisions
docs.text:
• JS 0.2.0 is forward-bound to Python develop unless Python 0.12.0 ships first.
  • JS 0.2.0 may ship before Python 0.12.0, but release notes must call out that users on Python 0.11.4 will not see the same root symbols yet.
  • BenchmarkClient / BenchmarkClientConfig are root-exported schema-deferred stubs.
  • Dataset and EvaluationExample are root and evaluation-subpath runtime exports.
  • EnterpriseAdminClient remains a throwing migration stub pending Traigent/traigent-js#30.
  • observability default-client helpers and observe move to 0.3.0.
  • the old Python-side public client ignore list should be replaced by the schema-owned manifest instead of duplicated.
  • manifest refresh happens on Python release candidates/release branches, Python traigent.__all__ changes, JS minor parity releases, and quarterly drift checks when release cadence is slow.

docs.title: Deferred Contract Decisions And Release Gating
docs.text:
The contract-policy detail for the three deferred surfaces lives in MIGRATION_FROM_PYTHON.md. The summary for roadmap planning is:

Surface Issue Current JS state Earliest target Gate to flip from deferred-* to matched EnterpriseAdminClient Traigent/traigent-js#30 throwing stub; REST-only migration path not in 0.2.x TraigentSchema-owned tenant/membership/SSO contract + Python __all__ export + cross-SDK oracle BenchmarkClient Traigent/traigent-js#31 throwing schema-deferred stub not in 0.2.x naming decision recorded + TraigentSchema-owned dataset-generation endpoint + parity-manifest reclass get_default_observability_client / set_default_observability_client / observe Traigent/traigent-js#38 not implemented, not exported 0.3.0 at earliest agreed default-client scope (per-process vs context-local) + observe semantics + tests

No JS minor release should ship any of these surfaces as matched without the parity-manifest reclassification landing in the same PR family. Adding a throwing stub for the observability helpers in 0.2.x is also out of scope, because it would commit the SDK to a runtime shape before the semantics are agreed.

docs.title: Migrating From The Python SDK
docs.text: This JS parity surface targets the canonical Python SDK in the Traigent repo. For the 0.2.0 parity release, the checked-in manifest pins the Python 0.12.0 public surface from the Traigent repo, not the older ../Traigent-reasoning v0.9.0 snapshot. Python SDK 0.12.0 has shipped and removed the Python-orchestrated JS bridge; new JS users should use this repo's docs/getting-started/minimal-integration.md and this migration guide.

docs.title: Release Target
docs.text: The JS parity work is intended for the next additive minor release of @traigent/sdk, 0.2.0. Existing optimization APIs remain backward compatible.

docs.title: Client Modules
docs.text:
Python imports map to JS subpath exports:

Python module JS subpath Main JS client traigent.projects @traigent/sdk/projects ProjectManagementClient traigent.prompts @traigent/sdk/prompts PromptManagementClient traigent.evaluation @traigent/sdk/evaluation EvaluationClient traigent.observability @traigent/sdk/observability ObservabilityClient traigent.core_metrics @traigent/sdk/core-metrics CoreMetricsClient traigent.enterprise @traigent/sdk/admin EnterpriseAdminClient

These clients are also exported from @traigent/sdk.

These clients target Traigent platform product APIs. They do not change the Hybrid Mode app-service REST contract used by external services implementing /config-space, /execute, and /evaluate.

EnterpriseAdminClient is exported as a migration stub because Python exposes it at package root today. Calling it throws a clear "not implemented in JS" error; use the Traigent REST API for enterprise admin operations until that surface is implemented. The implementation decision is tracked in Traigent/traigent-js#30 and the current policy is in Deferred Client Policy.

BenchmarkClient and BenchmarkClientConfig are also exported from the package root because Python develop re-added them. They are schema-deferred stubs in JS 0.2.0: calling generate(...) or generateSync(...) throws until TraigentSchema owns the benchmark dataset-generation endpoint contract. The product naming decision is tracked in Traigent/traigent-js#31; see Deferred Client Policy for gating criteria.

Dataset and EvaluationExample are lightweight JS runtime classes that accept both camelCase JS fields and Python-style input_data / expected_output fields. They are available from @traigent/sdk and @traigent/sdk/evaluation.

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
configure(options) updates process-wide JS SDK defaults. It is additive and idempotent: a second call merges over the previous defaults.

initialize(options) merges options into the global defaults and resolves a snapshot once. Clients constructed afterward use that snapshot unless a per-client option overrides it. If configure(...) is called after initialize(...), the new options are merged into the bound snapshot; later environment changes still do not leak in.

resetTraigentConfig() clears configured defaults and any initialized snapshot. It is primarily intended for tests and isolated scripts.

configure({ field: undefined }) is a no-op for that field. To clear a configured value, call resetTraigentConfig() and then configure the values you still want.

Configuration precedence is:

  1. environment variables
  2. configure() / initialize() defaults
  3. per-client or per-call options

Supported environment variables are:

  • TRAIGENT_BACKEND_URL
  • TRAIGENT_API_URL
  • TRAIGENT_API_KEY
  • TRAIGENT_JWT_TOKEN
  • TRAIGENT_TENANT_ID
  • TRAIGENT_PROJECT_ID
  • TRAIGENT_OFFLINE_MODE
  • TRAIGENT_RELEASE
  • TRAIGENT_ENVIRONMENT
  • TRAIGENT_OBSERVABILITY_PRIVACY_MODE
  • TRAIGENT_TVAR_OBSERVATION
  • TRAIGENT_PARENT_PID
  • TRAIGENT_IDLE_TIMEOUT_MS
  • TRAIGENT_ENABLE_EXPERIMENTAL_RUNTIME_SEAMLESS

Client config resolves as environment defaults, then configured defaults from configure(...) / initialize(...), then per-client or per-call options. Hybrid optimize/session helpers also accept explicit backendUrl and apiKey options over env defaults.

docs.title: Root Authoring Aliases
docs.text:
Python-style authoring aliases are available from @traigent/sdk:

  • Range(min, max, options?)
  • IntRange(min, max, options?)
  • LogRange(min, max, options?)
  • Choices(values, options?)
  • ConfigSpace(configurationSpace, options?)
  • when(predicate).then(predicate)
  • implies(condition, consequence)
  • requires(predicate)
  • require(predicate)

These are lightweight wrappers over the existing JS param.* and predicate constraint model. They are not a full port of Python's TVL expression-builder classes.

requires(predicate) and require(predicate) are authoring markers that return the predicate unchanged. They exist for readability and grep-ability, not for runtime validation or transformation.

Prefer requires(predicate) in JS code. require(predicate) is kept as a Python migration alias, but it can shadow CommonJS/global require in mixed CJS files and may trip downstream no-shadow lint rules.

For TypeScript annotations, use ConfigSpaceDefinition as the exported type name. ConfigSpace(...) is the value-side Python authoring helper.

docs.title: Schema And Casing
docs.text:
Backend DTO types are generated from ../TraigentSchema/traigent_schema/schemas by:

npm run generate:schema-types
npm run check:schema-types
npm run generate:version
npm run check:version

npm run build, npm run build:sdk, and prepublishOnly regenerate the SDK version constant before emitting bundles so local builds do not ship stale User-Agent telemetry.

The generated schema types are available from @traigent/sdk/schema. Public JS client methods expose camelCase types and convert to snake_case at the HTTP boundary.

Unknown request fields pass through after casing conversion except explicit security-sensitive keys such as nested auth tokens and tenant overrides. null values are preserved.

Most backend client methods expect the Python-style { data: ... } response envelope and unwrap it. Typed measure creation/update intentionally returns the raw backend object because the Python measures path also bypasses the product client envelope convention.

docs.title: Observability Parity
docs.text:
ObservabilityClient emits the same trace/observation field shape as the Python client: input_data, output_data, prompt_reference, correlation_ids, token counts, latency/cost fields, nested observations, and trace/session metadata. JS tests compare the emitted payload shape before it is submitted to the ingest endpoint.

Python develop also exposes default-client helpers (get_default_observability_client, set_default_observability_client) and an observe wrapper. Those are intentionally deferred out of JS 0.2.0 and tracked for 0.3.0 in Traigent/traigent-js#38 because they add new runtime semantics (process-global state and decorator-style instrumentation) beyond the client parity release. See Deferred Client Policy for the gating criteria.

docs.title: Deferred Client Policy
docs.text:
This section is the single source of truth for deferred or REST-only policy decisions covered by the schema-owned manifest and the linked JS tracking issues. JS 0.2.x intentionally does not implement the listed surfaces, and the parity manifest in TraigentSchema records each one as deferred-schema or deferred-backlog.

The general policy is the same for all three: the surface stays a stub (or absent) in @traigent/sdk until the upstream contract is stable. Stability requires, at minimum:

  1. an endpoint/contract owned by TraigentSchema/ (request/response shapes under TraigentSchema/traigent_schema/schemas),
  2. matching Python implementation in Traigent/traigent/ exported from traigent.__all__,
  3. parity-manifest reclassification from deferred-* to matched in ../TraigentSchema/parity/python-js-sdk.json,
  4. a JS minor release window large enough to ship the implementation with tests and a cross-SDK oracle update under CROSS_SDK_VALIDATION.md.

Reclassifying any of these to matched without all four conditions is a release-blocking error.

docs.title: EnterpriseAdminClient (Traigent/traigent-js#30)
docs.text:
• Current JS state: exported runtime symbol; every method throws UnsupportedActionError with a "use the Traigent REST API" hint.
  • Policy: REST-only until the enterprise admin surface (tenants, tenant memberships, tenant SSO config) is owned by a TraigentSchema contract that both Python and JS can consume. The JS stub remains throwing; the export is not removed because Python re-exports the symbol at package root and the parity manifest requires the name to exist.
  • Migration guidance for SDK users: call the Traigent REST API directly for tenant CRUD, tenant memberships, and tenant SSO config. The OpenAPI reference for the platform admin surface is owned by TraigentBackend/ and the schemas live under TraigentSchema/traigent_schema/schemas. The SDK does not bundle a wrapper, fallback HTTP client, or REST helper for these endpoints in 0.2.x.
  • Release gating: implementation is not in scope for any 0.2.x release. A future minor release may pick up the surface only after the four stability conditions above are met. Until then, do not add new methods, rename methods, or change the stub error message in a way that could read as a real implementation.

docs.title: BenchmarkClient (Traigent/traigent-js#31)
docs.text:
• Current JS state: exported runtime symbol with two stub methods, generate(...) and generateSync(...), both throwing UnsupportedActionError referencing the deferred dataset-generation endpoint.
  • Policy: schema-deferred until both (a) the Python and JS public surface agree on whether benchmark functionality ships as BenchmarkClient, a differently named client, or no root client at all, and (b) TraigentSchema owns the dataset-generation request/response contract. The JS export is retained so the parity-manifest guard does not flap when Python toggles the symbol on traigent.__all__.
  • Migration guidance for SDK users: until the contract lands, use the existing EvaluationClient and the Dataset / EvaluationExample runtime classes to assemble evaluation datasets locally, or call the platform REST surface for any pre-built datasets the backend exposes. Do not depend on any specific BenchmarkClient method name, return type, or import path in application code; they may change when the naming decision lands.
  • Release gating: no implementation in 0.2.x. The first JS release that ships a working benchmark client must (a) land after the TraigentSchema-owned endpoint, (b) match the agreed product name from Traigent/traigent-js#31, and (c) update the parity manifest entry from deferred-schema to matched in the same family of PRs.

docs.title: Default observability helpers and span wrappers (Traigent/traigent-js#38)
docs.text:
• Current JS state: not implemented and not exported. The exported ObservabilityClient class is the only entry point; consumers construct it explicitly per use site.
  • Policy: deferred to 0.3.0 at the earliest. The Python helpers get_default_observability_client, set_default_observability_client, and observe, plus the broader add_agent_span style helper family, introduce process-global state and decorator/span instrumentation semantics that the 0.2.0 client-parity release intentionally avoided. They will not be added in 0.2.x even as stubs, because adding throwing global accessors would commit the SDK to a runtime shape before the semantics are agreed.
  • Migration guidance for SDK users: construct an ObservabilityClient explicitly at module load time and pass it through application code, or call the observability REST routes through the existing client. For decorator-style instrumentation, wrap the target function manually with startTrace / recordObservation / endTrace; do not rely on the Python-side global helper shape carrying over verbatim.
  • Release gating: target window is @traigent/sdk 0.3.0, contingent on agreement about (a) where the default client lives (per-process singleton vs. per-context via the existing TrialContext), (b) whether observe binds to that default or to a passed-in client, and (c) error and async semantics for the wrapped function. None of those decisions are settled in 0.2.0, so 0.3.0 cannot land before they are.

docs.title: Assisted authoring and onboarding backlog
docs.text:
• Current JS state: Python-only today. JS does not expose catalog recommendation APIs, strategy presets, advisory selection helpers, or an onboarding CLI.
  • Policy: deferred-backlog. These surfaces combine product guidance, benchmark/objective recommendation, and CLI workflow semantics; they should only move into JS after the product contract, schema classification, and cross-SDK oracle coverage are settled.
  • Migration guidance for SDK users: author JS configuration spaces directly with param.*, ConfigSpace(...), Range(...), IntRange(...), LogRange(...), and Choices(...), or use platform documentation/workflows outside this package for recommendation and onboarding guidance.

docs.title: Snapshot stability
docs.text: The runtime API snapshot at tests/integration/fixtures/api-surface.snapshot.json and the parity-clients test at tests/unit/platform/parity-clients.test.ts are the load-bearing checks for the policy in this section. If you reclassify any of the three surfaces, update the snapshot and the test in the same PR.

docs.title: Metric Edge Cases
docs.text:
The optimization surface follows these parity rules:

  • evaluation.metric is an accuracy-style primary metric named accuracy.
  • evaluation.metrics / evaluation.metricFunctions derive the first metric name as the primary metric when objectives are omitted.
  • Boolean metric returns are normalized to 1 or 0.
  • null metric values are preserved through aggregation; objectives still require a finite numeric value for best-config selection.
  • A conflicting metric definition means the same metric key is provided by both evaluation.metrics and evaluation.metricFunctions.

docs.title: Error Mapping
docs.text:
Python exception JS error Retryable AuthenticationError AuthenticationError no ClientError ClientError only 408, 429, or 5xx TraigentConnectionError TraigentConnectionError yes validation/config errors ValidationError no unsupported operation UnsupportedActionError no

For rejected HTTP authentication/authorization responses, the parsed or raw server response body is available at error.details.body.

docs.title: Validation Command
docs.text:
Use the bundled CI command for parity changes:

npm run test:ci

It includes schema freshness, SDK-version freshness, the schema-owned Python/JS parity manifest check, format, lint, typecheck, coverage, build, and package smoke checks for every subpath export in both ESM and CJS.

The authoritative manifest lives in the sibling TraigentSchema repo at:

../TraigentSchema/parity/python-js-sdk.json

The JS checker pins only the Python SHA and verifies this package satisfies the manifest-required root exports:

npm run build:sdk
npm run check:parity-manifest

Manifest refresh cadence:

  • refresh on every Python public SDK release candidate or release branch
  • refresh in the same PR family as any Python change to traigent.__all__
  • refresh before every JS minor parity release
  • if neither SDK releases for a quarter, run a quarterly drift check and open a no-op or update PR documenting the result

TVL fixtures remain owned by the sibling ../tvl repo. Do not copy divergent fixtures into this package; vendor them only via a script with checksum verification when needed.

docs.title: Native JS Parity Matrix
docs.text:
This matrix describes the verified state of the native-first JS checkout in this repository root.

Use it together with:

  • Python SDK Module Catalog and Gap Analysis
  • JS Parity Roadmap
  • Hybrid JS parity status is summarized in docs/HYBRID_JS_PARITY_MATRIX.md.

Important scope note:

  • this file is about the native execution path in the current checkout
  • call-level backend-guided hybrid Optuna exists in this repo, but spec-level execution.mode = "hybrid" is still rejected

docs.title: Label Semantics
docs.text:
• matched: implemented in this checkout and covered by passing tests or a verified public example

  • gap: no backend change is required; JS could implement it in this project today
  • deferred-backend: blocked on hybrid/backend protocol or control-plane work
  • out-of-scope: not a target for the native JS SDK

docs.title: Python Parity Client Live Smoke
docs.text: Run this smoke against a dedicated staging tenant and project before publishing @traigent/sdk 0.2.0. Do not run it against production data.

docs.title: Required Environment
docs.text:
export TRAIGENT_BACKEND_URL="https://staging.example"
export TRAIGENT_API_KEY="..."
export TRAIGENT_PROJECT_ID="..."
export TRAIGENT_TENANT_ID="..." # if required by the tenant
export TRAIGENT_SMOKE_ALLOW_WRITE=1

Use TRAIGENT_API_URL instead of TRAIGENT_BACKEND_URL if the staging backend expects a fully scoped API URL.

docs.title: Staging Environment Source
docs.text:
The source of truth for release smoke configuration is the parity-client-smoke GitHub Environment in Traigent/traigent-js. Configure these environment secrets there:

  • TRAIGENT_BACKEND_URL or TRAIGENT_API_URL
  • TRAIGENT_API_KEY
  • TRAIGENT_PROJECT_ID
  • TRAIGENT_TENANT_ID if required by the staging tenant

Do not commit a .env.smoke file with real values. For local release runs, populate the shell from the same approved staging secret source.

The smoke refuses https://api.traigent.ai and https://portal.traigent.ai by default because the observability step writes one trace. Only set TRAIGENT_SMOKE_ALLOW_PROD=1 for an intentional production diagnostic run.

docs.title: Command
docs.text:
npm run smoke:parity-clients

After this workflow exists on the default branch, the same release gate can be run manually through GitHub Actions with the Parity Client Smoke workflow.

docs.title: Smoke Surface
docs.text:
The script verifies these Python-parity clients against the built SDK:

  • ProjectManagementClient.listProjects()
  • PromptManagementClient.listPrompts()
  • EvaluationClient.listEvaluators()
  • ObservabilityClient.startTrace(), endTrace(), and flush() with one minimal trace
  • CoreMetricsClient.getCoreMetricsOverview()

EnterpriseAdminClient is intentionally skipped because it is a migration stub in JS 0.2.0 and throws UnsupportedActionError.

If the staging project has no metrics data yet, set TRAIGENT_SMOKE_SKIP_CORE_METRICS=1 and record that exception in the release notes. All other steps are publish gates.

docs.title: Failure Policy
docs.text:
Do not publish 0.2.0 if any required smoke step fails. Investigate whether the failure is a route mismatch, auth/tenant scoping issue, envelope mismatch, or a real backend availability problem.

If 0.2.0 ships and a critical SDK bug is found after publish, deprecate the bad npm version with a clear message and ship a 0.2.1 patch. Critical means a P0 parity-client issue such as silent data loss, auth bypass, a request routed to the wrong tenant, or import-time/runtime failure on the new client surface. Do not rely on unpublish as the rollback plan.

docs.title: Python SDK Module Catalog and Gap Analysis
docs.text:
This document audits the canonical Python SDK package under:

  • Traigent/traigent

It has two purposes:

  1. create a complete module-family catalog using a consistent format
  2. compare those Python capabilities to the current JS SDK project state

Branch-aware reading guide:

  • matched: implemented and covered by passing tests or a verified public example
  • partial: implemented with bounded semantics and covered, but still behind Python
  • gap: the backend/API contract is reachable today and the JS side could implement it now
  • deferred-backend: blocked on missing or insufficiently specified backend/protocol support
  • out-of-scope: not a current JS SDK target

2026-06 parity update:

  • The canonical parity source is the Python SDK 0.12.0 public surface in the Traigent repo. ../../Traigent-reasoning` is a v0.9.0 snapshot and is not a parity target.
  • JS now ships Python-aligned subpath clients for projects, prompts, evaluation, observability, and core_metrics.
  • EnterpriseAdminClient is exported as a migration stub only; enterprise admin operations remain a REST/API-level gap in JS.
  • See MIGRATION_FROM_PYTHON.md for the current migration contract, config precedence, generated schema flow, and error map.
  • The schema-owned manifest keeps catalog recommendation APIs, strategy presets, add_agent_span, onboarding CLI, default observability helpers, and advisory selection helpers out of JS 0.2.x parity.

Bridge removal note:

  • This catalog captured the Python v0.11.4 module inventory. Python SDK 0.12.0 removes traigent.bridges, JSEvaluator, and the ExecutionOptions.runtime / ExecutionOptions.js_* fields. Treat bridge rows and bridge file-inventory entries in this document as Python SDK <= 0.11.x historical context, not current parity targets.

docs.title: Scope and Counting Method
docs.text:
• Root audited: Traigent/traigent
  • Included: tracked package artifacts under that root (.py, .md,.yaml,py.typed`, root package files)
  • Excluded: __pycache__, .mypy_cache
  • Total tracked package artifacts covered: 366

docs.title: Documentation Format
docs.text:
Each catalog row uses the same fields:

  • Category: top-level package family
  • Label: concise functional label
  • Description: what the family does
  • Key Types / APIs: main public or architectural surfaces
  • Responsibility: what this family owns in the Python SDK
  • File Count: tracked files under that family
  • Native Status: current state of this native-first checkout
  • Hybrid Status: current state of this repo's hybrid/session surface
  • Overall JS Status: project-wide status across both active JS lines
  • Evidence: passing tests or verified public examples for matched / partial rows

docs.title: Areas Where Current JS Is Strong
docs.text:
• Public optimize API and high-level agent optimization mental model
  • Native runtime search algorithms: grid, random, local Bayesian
  • Evaluation loop ownership in native mode
  • Explicit injection modes: context, parameter, and practical seamless
  • Cost and stop-condition handling for native-local optimization
  • Basic framework interception for OpenAI / LangChain / Vercel AI, plus autoWrapFrameworkTarget(...) / autoWrapFrameworkTargets({...}) helpers and bounded explicit-object discovery via discoverFrameworkTargets(...) / prepareFrameworkTargets(...)
  • DTO/measure validation and native runtime coverage

docs.title: Areas Where Python Still Exceeds This JS Checkout
1. Cloud and Hybrid Execution
docs.text:
Python owns a full remote stack across:

  • Traigent/traigent/cloud
  • Traigent/traigent/hybrid
  • parts of Traigent/traigent/core
  • parts of Traigent/traigent/optimizers

Current JS project:

  • native-first in this checkout
  • backend-guided hybrid execution exists through this repo's typed session surface
  • that surface now includes:
  • typed session lifecycle
  • low-level typed session helpers for create / next-trial / submit-result
  • list / health / status / finalize / delete helpers with normalized DTOs
  • high-level plain-agent evaluation
  • backend-guided config suggestion
  • OpenAI / LangChain / Vercel AI seamless interception with runtime metric collection
  • fully targets backend-guided local execution over the typed /sessions surface
  • does not target Python's server-side remote execution model, where agents are reconstructed and invoked in the cloud
  • therefore, the remaining Python cloud-execution families should be treated as out-of-scope for JS unless that product decision changes

docs.title: 2. TVL
docs.text:
Python has a full TVL language/runtime:

  • spec loading
  • models
  • validation
  • promotion policy
  • banded/statistical objectives
  • TVL CLI

Current JS checkout:

  • implements a focused native TVL subset:
  • typed tvars
  • banded objectives
  • structural and derived constraints compiled to callbacks through a parsed safe-expression subset
  • exploration strategy/budget mapping
  • promotion-policy parsing
  • artifact-specific nativeCompatibility reporting on loaded TVL artifacts, including usedFeatures and summarized warnings
  • native TVL CLI inspection via traigent inspect tvl ...
  • still does not implement the full Python TVL runtime or CLI, and it does not expose the full Python promotion-gate lifecycle/reporting model; native JS now applies minEffect and tieBreakers during best-trial selection, uses sample-based paired promotion / TOST band promotion when trials expose metric samples, chanceConstraints can reject trials when they provide explicit counts or binary metric samples, and native results/trials now expose bounded promotionDecision reports

docs.title: 3. Cloud Clients, Sessions, and Control Plane
docs.text:
Python includes:

  • session lifecycle APIs
  • DTO/model contracts
  • subset selection
  • billing/privacy/auth operations
  • sync/resilience layers

Current JS project:

  • typed interactive session orchestration exists in this repo
  • that surface now covers the practical decorator path better than before:
  • plain agent functions
  • local evaluation
  • seamless framework overrides
  • framework auto-wrap helpers, bounded explicit-object discovery, and seamless diagnostics
  • low-level typed session helpers for create / next-trial / submit-result
  • session list / status / finalize / delete helpers with normalized DTOs
  • executable hybrid session-control example
  • provider-derived cost/token/latency submission
  • now covers the reachable typed /sessions control-plane surface well
  • still lacks Python's broader cloud/session/control-plane surface, resilience layers, and DTO breadth where those depend on:
  • backend routes that are not exposed on the current typed /sessions surface, or
  • Python's remote cloud-execution model, which JS does not target

docs.title: 4. Config Generation and Assisted Authoring
docs.text:
Python includes:

  • agent classification
  • benchmark catalogs
  • objective recommendation
  • safety-constraint recommendation
  • tuned-variable range generation
  • catalog recommendation APIs
  • strategy presets and advisory selection helpers
  • onboarding CLI

Current JS checkout:

  • no equivalent authoring-generation pipeline
  • these remain schema-manifest deferred backlog items, not JS 0.2.x parity claims

docs.title: 5. Enterprise Security / Platform Concerns
docs.text:
Python package contains an enterprise/platform layer:

  • auth
  • JWT/OIDC/SAML/MFA
  • encryption
  • rate limiting
  • tenancy
  • session security

Current JS checkout:

  • not a product target for the local SDK package
  • divergence is justified and expected

docs.title: Divergences That Are Justified
docs.text:
• Python has platform/security/analytics families that should not be copied directly into the JS SDK package.
  • Python cloud/hybrid clients should be reconciled against reachable backend session contracts, not Python-only remote execution surfaces.
  • Python's remote cloud-execution APIs are not a JS parity target; JS hybrid is explicitly backend-guided local execution.
  • TVL parity in JS should be deliberate; it should not be copied blindly if the JS user-facing authoring API is clearer.

docs.title: Gaps That Are Not Yet Justified
docs.text:
• full hybrid/cloud session parity should be reconciled against the reachable typed-session portions of Traigent/traigent/cloud and Traigent/traigent/hybrid, not Python's remote cloud-execution-only surfaces
  • TVL support remains a meaningful Python-to-JS feature gap if the long-term goal is cross-SDK authoring parity
  • tuned-variable discovery is now partially covered in the native checkout through bounded heuristics and a CLI surface, but both discovery breadth and config-generation parity are still materially behind Python

docs.title: Recommended Next Work
docs.text:
1. Treat this doc as the canonical category map for Python-to-JS parity discussions.
  2. Keep native JS work focused on api, config, core, optimizers, evaluators, integrations, metrics, and selected utils.
  3. Use the hybrid JS branch to close the cloud and hybrid gaps, not this native-first checkout.
  4. If cross-SDK authoring parity matters, bring over TVL in a deliberate phase instead of re-encoding it ad hoc.
  5. Consider a second pass that maps every Python file to a concrete JS file or explicit deferred/out-of-scope decision.

docs.title: Real Production Mode Execution - Sequence Flow
docs.text:
Archived legacy reference. This document describes the older Python-to-Node bridge and CLI runner flow. Python SDK 0.12.0 removed the runtime="node" / js_* execution options, so new JS integrations should use JS-native wrapped.optimize(...) or hybrid spec authoring instead. The runner is retained temporarily for Python SDK <= 0.11.x compatibility until the Stage 2 cleanup.

For the current JS-facing API, use `README.md and CLIENT_CODE_GUIDE.md.

docs.title: Mermaid Sequence Diagram
docs.text:
sequenceDiagram
    participant User as User Script
    participant Dec as @traigent.optimize
    participant OF as OptimizedFunction
    participant Orch as Orchestrator
    participant Opt as Optimizer
    participant Eval as JSEvaluator
    participant Pool as JSProcessPool
    participant Bridge as JSBridge
    participant Runner as CLI Runner (Node.js)
    participant Trial as runTrial()
    participant Agent as Agent/LLM
    participant Backend as Backend API

    %% Initialization
    User->>Dec: @traigent.optimize(execution={runtime:"node"})
    Dec->>OF: OptimizedFunction(func, config_space, ...)
    User->>OF: await optimize()

    %% Setup
    OF->>Opt: get_optimizer("random", max_trials=24)
    OF->>Pool: JSProcessPool(max_workers=4, module_path)
    Pool->>Bridge: JSBridge(config) [x4 workers]

    %% Worker startup
    loop For each worker
        Bridge->>Runner: subprocess_exec("npx traigent-js --module ...")
        Runner->>Runner: loadTrialFunction(module, "runTrial")
        Runner->>Runner: startParentPidWatcher()
        Runner->>Runner: rl = createInterface(stdin)
        Bridge->>Runner: ping request (NDJSON)
        Runner-->>Bridge: pong response (NDJSON)
    end

    OF->>Eval: JSEvaluator(process_pool=pool)
    OF->>Orch: OptimizationOrchestrator(optimizer, evaluator)

    %% Trial Loop
    loop While not should_stop
        Orch->>Opt: suggest() → config
        Note over Opt: config = {model, temperature, ...}

        Orch->>Eval: evaluate(func, config, dataset)
        Eval->>Eval: _build_trial_config(trial_id, config, indices)
        Eval->>Pool: run_trial(trial_config)
        Pool->>Bridge: acquire() → worker

        %% NDJSON Protocol
        Bridge->>Runner: run_trial request (NDJSON stdin)
        Note over Bridge,Runner: {"action":"run_trial","payload":{...}}

        Runner->>Runner: parseRequest() + Zod validation
        Runner->>Runner: acquireTrialLock()
        Runner->>Runner: currentTrialId = trial_id
        Runner->>Trial: TrialContext.run(config, trialFn)

        Trial->>Trial: getDatasetSubset(indices)
        Trial->>Agent: runSalesAgent(examples, agentConfig)

        alt REAL_MODE=true
            Agent->>Agent: realLLMCall(model, messages)
            Note over Agent: Actual Groq/OpenAI API call
        else Mock Mode
            Agent->>Agent: simulateMetrics(config)
        end

        Agent-->>Trial: {metrics, latency, tokens, cost}
        Trial-->>Runner: {metrics, duration, metadata}

        Runner->>Runner: sanitizeMeasures(metrics)
        Runner->>Runner: createSuccessResult(trial_id, metrics)
        Runner-->>Bridge: success response (NDJSON stdout)
        Note over Runner,Bridge: {"status":"success","payload":{metrics}}

        Bridge->>Bridge: _parse_trial_response() → JSTrialResult
        Pool->>Bridge: release(worker)
        Bridge-->>Eval: JSTrialResult

        Eval->>Eval: _convert_js_result() → EvaluationResult
        Eval-->>Orch: EvaluationResult

        Orch->>Opt: report(config, metrics)
        Orch->>Orch: _update_best(config, result)
        Orch->>Backend: submit_trial(trial_result)
    end

    %% Shutdown
    Orch-->>OF: OptimizationResult
    OF->>Pool: shutdown()
    Pool->>Bridge: stop() [x4 workers]
    Bridge->>Runner: shutdown request (NDJSON)
    Runner->>Runner: process.exit(0)
    OF-->>User: OptimizationResult(best_config, best_metrics)

async def start(self) -> None:
    cmd = ["npx", "traigent-js", "--module", module_path, "--function", "runTrial"]

    self._process = await asyncio.create_subprocess_exec(
        *cmd,
        stdin=asyncio.subprocess.PIPE,
        stdout=asyncio.subprocess.PIPE,
        stderr=asyncio.subprocess.PIPE,
        start_new_session=True,
    )

    self._reader_task = asyncio.create_task(self._read_responses())
    await self.ping(timeout=10.0)  # Health check

async function main(): Promise<void> {
  const args = parseCLIArgs();

  trialFn = await loadTrialFunction(args.module, args.function ?? 'runTrial');

  startParentPidWatcher();
  resetIdleTimer();

  rl = createInterface({ input: process.stdin, crlfDelay: Infinity });

  rl.on('line', (line) => {
    processRequest(line, trialFn);
  });
}

async function processRequest(line: string, trialFn: UserTrialFunction): Promise<void> {
  const lineBytes = Buffer.byteLength(line, 'utf8');
  if (lineBytes > MAX_PAYLOAD_SIZE) {
    sendResponse(
      createErrorResponse('unknown', 'Payload too large', { errorCode: 'PAYLOAD_TOO_LARGE' })
    );
    return;
  }

  const request = parseRequest(line); // Zod validation

  switch (request.action) {
    case 'run_trial':
      response = await handleRunTrial(request, trialFn);
      break;
    // ...
  }

  sendResponse(response);
}

Parse Request: src/cli/protocol.ts#L275-L284

docs.title: Phase 10: Trial Execution with Lock
docs.text:
Handle Run Trial: src/cli/runner.ts#L267-L401

async function handleRunTrial(request: CLIRequest, trialFn: UserTrialFunction): Promise<CLIResponse> {
  const trialStartTime = Date.now();

  // 1. Acquire lock
  const unlock = await acquireTrialLock();

  // 2. Check busy
  if (currentTrialId !== null) {
    unlock();
    return createErrorResponse(request.request_id, new BusyError(...));
  }

  // 3. Validate with Zod
  const parseResult = TrialConfigSchema.safeParse(request.payload);

  // 4. Set state IMMEDIATELY
  currentTrialId = config.trial_id;
  currentTrialAbortController = new AbortController();

  try {
    // 5. Execute with context
    const result = await TrialContext.run(config, async () => {
      return await trialFn(config);
    }, abortSignal);

    // 6. Sanitize & respond
    const metrics = sanitizeMeasures(result.metrics);
    return createSuccessResponse(request.request_id, createSuccessResult(...));
  } finally {
    currentTrialId = null;
    unlock();
  }
}

Key Functions: | Function | Location | Purpose | |----------|----------|---------| | acquireTrialLock | src/cli/runner.ts#L76-L84 | Race condition prevention | | TrialContext.run | src/core/context.ts | AsyncLocalStorage context | | sanitizeMeasures | src/dtos/measures.ts | Metric validation |

export async function runTrial(trialConfig: TrialConfig): Promise<TrialResult> {
  const agentConfig: AgentConfig = {
    model: trialConfig.config.model ?? 'gpt-4o-mini',
    temperature: trialConfig.config.temperature ?? 0.5,
    system_prompt: trialConfig.config.system_prompt ?? 'consultative',
    memory_turns: trialConfig.config.memory_turns ?? 5,
    tool_set: trialConfig.config.tool_set ?? 'standard',
  };

  const examples = getDatasetSubset(trialConfig.dataset_subset.indices);
  const result = await runSalesAgent(examples, agentConfig, console.error);

  return {
    metrics: {
      accuracy: (result.avg_relevancy + result.avg_completeness) / 2,
      response_time: result.avg_latency_ms,
      conversion_score: result.avg_conversion_score,
      cost: result.total_cost,
      margin_efficiency: result.margin_efficiency,
      // ...
    },
    duration: Date.now() - startTime,
  };
}

// When REAL_MODE=true
export async function realLLMCall(config: LLMConfig): Promise<LLMResponse> {
  const response = await fetch('https://api.groq.com/...', {
    method: 'POST',
    headers: { Authorization: `Bearer ${GROQ_API_KEY}` },
    body: JSON.stringify({
      model: config.model, // "groq/llama-3.1-8b-instant"
      temperature: config.temperature,
      messages: config.messages,
    }),
  });

  return {
    content: response.choices[0].message.content,
    usage: { input_tokens, output_tokens, cost },
    latency: responseTime,
  };
}

docs.title: Phase 13: Response Back to Python
docs.text:
Send Response: src/cli/runner.ts#L213-L235

function sendResponse(response: CLIResponse): void {
  const line = serializeResponse(response) + '\n';

  if (draining) {
    writeQueue.push(line);
    return;
  }

  const ok = process.stdout.write(line);
  if (!ok) {
    draining = true;
    rl?.pause(); // Backpressure
    process.stdout.once('drain', flushWriteQueue);
  }
}

  "request_id": "abc123-uuid",
  "status": "success",
  "payload": {
    "trial_id": "js_trial_4ffe457b",
